decisions.title: Move to usage-based billing in Q4
decisions.statement: Migrate our B2B SaaS product from per-seat pricing to usage-based billing in Q4.
decisions.context: Flat NRR, sales says seats are capping deal sizes, two competitors moved to usage-based this year.

assumptions.text: Enough current customers will accept usage-based pricing at renewal to protect NRR.
assumptions.signpost: Renewal conversations where procurement rejects or escalates usage-based terms.

assumptions.text: Metering infrastructure can be production-ready before the Q4 billing cutover.
assumptions.signpost: Billing team timeline estimates slipping past the 5-week plan or new cross-team dependencies.

assumptions.text: Usage-based pricing will expand deal sizes in the current sales pipeline.
assumptions.signpost: Late-stage deals not asking about usage pricing, or AEs reporting no expansion effect.

assumptions.text: The market shift toward usage-based pricing in our category will continue.
assumptions.signpost: Competitors walking back usage pricing or reintroducing seat-based elements.

evidence.text: Renewal call with Meridian: CFO said per-seat is the only pricing model their procurement will approve this fiscal year. Usage-based would push renewal to committee.
Competitor B walked back part of its usage pricing after churn in mid-market accounts, reintroducing a seat-based floor.
Billing team estimates metering infrastructure at 9 weeks, not the 5 in the original plan. Two dependencies on the platform team.
Sales pipeline review: 3 of 7 late-stage deals asked about usage pricing unprompted. AEs believe it would have expanded 2 of them.

decisions.title: Outsource fulfillment to a 3PL by Q2
decisions.statement: Move e-commerce fulfillment from our in-house warehouse to a third-party logistics provider (3PL) by Q2.
decisions.context: Order volume up 60 percent year over year; warehouse lease renewal due in 8 months; 3PL quotes suggest 18 percent cost savings at current volume.

assumptions.text: The 3PL can match our in-house shipping speed and accuracy closely enough that customers don't notice.
assumptions.signpost: Pilot same-day-ship rate or mispick rate diverging from in-house baselines; shipping complaints rising.

assumptions.text: The quoted 18 percent cost savings will hold at our actual volumes and SKU count.
assumptions.signpost: Pricing-tier triggers in the contract, such as SKU count or volume thresholds, coming into play.

assumptions.text: The 3PL will remain stable and able to onboard us fully before our warehouse lease decision.
assumptions.signpost: Ownership changes, onboarding freezes, or client-reported service disruptions at the 3PL.

assumptions.text: Our product data feed is clean enough to drive a third party's pick-and-pack operation.
assumptions.signpost: Fulfillment errors traced to catalog or data-feed issues rather than 3PL execution.

evidence.text: 3PL pilot, week 3: 94 percent of orders shipped same-day vs our 97 percent in-house. Two mispicks on bundled SKUs, both traced to our product data feed.
Post-purchase survey trend: 'fast shipping' mentions in 5-star reviews dropped from 31 percent to 24 percent during the pilot region rollout.
The 3PL announced acquisition by a larger logistics group; two of their mid-size clients publicly flagged onboarding freezes during the transition.
Ops review: 3PL pricing assumes our current SKU count. Planned Q3 product line expansion adds 40 SKUs, which triggers their next pricing tier.

decisions.title: Launch a self-serve tier in January
decisions.statement: Launch a self-serve tier in January to expand from enterprise into mid-market without growing the sales team.
decisions.context: CAC on enterprise deals rising; board wants efficient growth; product currently requires white-glove onboarding.

assumptions.text: Mid-market buyers in our category will convert through a purely self-serve funnel.
assumptions.signpost: Category benchmarks or our own trials showing sales-assisted conversion far above self-serve.

assumptions.text: Onboarding can be automated enough that customers connect their data without human help.
assumptions.signpost: Trial drop-off concentrated at the data-connection step; automation project slipping.

assumptions.text: Self-serve customers can be supported profitably at mid-market price points.
assumptions.signpost: Support ticket volume per customer staying at levels that break unit economics below target ARPU.

assumptions.text: A January launch window is achievable with the current engineering roadmap.
assumptions.signpost: Onboarding-automation completion percentage stalling through Q4.

evidence.text: Industry report: mid-market buyers in this category still convert 3x better with sales-assisted trials than pure self-serve.
Trial exit survey, n=41: top drop-off reason was 'couldn't connect our data without help' (58 percent).
Onboarding automation project is 70 percent complete; the remaining 30 percent covers exactly the data-connection step.
Support lead: current ticket volume per customer would make self-serve unprofitable below $400/mo unless deflection improves.
